title: Git interactive rebase for cleaning up commit history before merge
body:
Interactive rebase lets you squash, reorder, edit, and drop commits before merging a feature branch. This produces a clean, readable history.

```bash
# Rebase the last 5 commits
git rebase -i HEAD~5

# Rebase onto main (all commits since divergence)
git rebase -i main
```

The editor opens with a commit list:
```
pick a1b2c3d Add user model
pick e4f5g6h Fix typo in user model
pick i7j8k9l Add user API endpoint
pick m0n1o2p Fix lint error
pick q3r4s5t Add user tests
```

Common operations:
```
pick a1b2c3d Add user model
fixup e4f5g6h Fix typo in user model     # Merge into previous, discard message
pick i7j8k9l Add user API endpoint
fixup m0n1o2p Fix lint error              # Merge into previous
pick q3r4s5t Add user tests
```

Result: 3 clean commits instead of 5.

**Commands available:**
- `pick` — keep the commit as-is
- `reword` — keep the commit, edit message
- `squash` — merge into previous commit, combine messages
- `fixup` — merge into previous commit, discard message
- `drop` — remove the commit entirely
- `edit` — pause rebase to amend the commit

**If conflicts occur during rebase:**
```bash
# Resolve conflicts in files, then:
git add .
git rebase --continue

# Or abort the entire rebase:
git rebase --abort
```

Never rebase commits that have been pushed to a shared branch. Rebase rewrites history, which forces teammates to reconcile divergent histories.

title: Git cherry-pick commits across branches with conflict resolution
body:
Cherry-pick applies specific commits from one branch to another. Useful for backporting fixes or selectively moving changes.

```bash
# Cherry-pick a single commit
git cherry-pick abc1234

# Cherry-pick multiple commits
git cherry-pick abc1234 def5678 ghi9012

# Cherry-pick a range (exclusive start, inclusive end)
git cherry-pick main~5..main~2

# Cherry-pick without committing (stage changes only)
git cherry-pick --no-commit abc1234

# Cherry-pick a merge commit (specify parent)
git cherry-pick -m 1 abc1234  # -m 1 = first parent (usually main)
```

**Handling conflicts:**
```bash
# After resolving conflicts:
git add resolved-file.ts
git cherry-pick --continue

# Abort cherry-pick:
git cherry-pick --abort

# Skip this commit and continue with remaining:
git cherry-pick --skip
```

**Common workflow — backport a fix to a release branch:**
```bash
# On main, find the fix commit
git log --oneline --grep="fix: login validation"
# abc1234 fix: login validation bypass (CVE-2024-1234)

# Apply to release branch
git checkout release/2.1
git cherry-pick abc1234
git push origin release/2.1
```

Cherry-pick creates a new commit with a different SHA but the same changes. Use `git cherry-pick -x abc1234` to append "(cherry picked from commit abc1234)" to the commit message for traceability.

title: Git bisect for finding the commit that introduced a bug
body:
Git bisect performs a binary search through commit history to find exactly which commit introduced a bug. It halves the search space each step.

```bash
# Start bisect
git bisect start

# Mark current commit as bad (has the bug)
git bisect bad

# Mark a known good commit (before the bug existed)
git bisect good v2.0.0  # or a commit SHA

# Git checks out a commit in the middle. Test it, then:
git bisect good  # if this commit works fine
# or
git bisect bad   # if this commit has the bug

# Repeat until Git finds the first bad commit
# Bisecting: 3 revisions left to test after this (roughly 2 steps)
# abc1234 is the first bad commit

# End bisect and return to original branch
git bisect reset
```

**Automated bisect with a test script:**
```bash
# Automatically run a test at each step
git bisect start HEAD v2.0.0
git bisect run npm test -- --testPathPattern="login.test"

# Or with a custom script
git bisect run ./test-login.sh
# Script must exit 0 for good, 1-124/126-127 for bad, 125 for skip
```

**test-login.sh:**
```bash
#!/bin/bash
npm install --silent 2>/dev/null
npm test -- --testPathPattern="login" 2>/dev/null
```

With 1000 commits between good and bad, bisect finds the culprit in ~10 steps (log2(1000)). Automated bisect can find bugs while you grab coffee. Use `git bisect skip` if a commit can't be tested (e.g., broken build unrelated to the bug).

title: Git worktrees for working on multiple branches simultaneously
body:
Git worktrees let you check out multiple branches at once in separate directories, sharing the same repository. No more stashing or committing WIP.

```bash
# List existing worktrees
git worktree list
# /Users/dev/myapp        abc1234 [main]

# Create a worktree for a feature branch
git worktree add ../myapp-feature-auth feature/auth
# Now you can cd ../myapp-feature-auth and work on feature/auth

# Create a worktree for a new branch
git worktree add -b hotfix/login ../myapp-hotfix main
# Creates new branch 'hotfix/login' from 'main'

# Create a worktree for reviewing a PR
git worktree add ../myapp-pr-review pr-branch

# Remove a worktree when done
git worktree remove ../myapp-feature-auth

# Force remove (if worktree has changes)
git worktree remove --force ../myapp-hotfix

# Prune stale worktree entries
git worktree prune
```

**Typical workflow:**
```bash
# Main development
cd ~/projects/myapp
# Working on feature/dashboard

# Urgent hotfix needed — don't stash, create worktree
git worktree add ../myapp-hotfix -b hotfix/payment main
cd ../myapp-hotfix
# Fix the bug, commit, push
git push -u origin hotfix/payment

# Return to feature work
cd ~/projects/myapp
# Everything is exactly where you left it

# Clean up after hotfix is merged
git worktree remove ../myapp-hotfix
```

Worktrees share the `.git` directory, so fetches, config, and stashes are shared. You cannot have the same branch checked out in two worktrees. Each worktree has its own working directory and index.

title: Turborepo configuration for monorepo build orchestration
ESLint flat config migration from legacy .eslintrc format
Prettier configuration with Tailwind CSS plugin and import sorting
body:
Prettier auto-formats code. The Tailwind plugin sorts utility classes in a canonical order. Import sorting replaces ESLint's import/order for formatting.

```javascript
// prettier.config.js
/** @type {import('prettier').Config} */
export default {
  semi: true,
  singleQuote: true,
  trailingComma: 'all',
  printWidth: 100,
  tabWidth: 2,
  arrowParens: 'always',
  endOfLine: 'lf',
  plugins: [
    'prettier-plugin-tailwindcss',
    '@ianvs/prettier-plugin-sort-imports',
  ],

  // Tailwind class sorting
  tailwindFunctions: ['cn', 'clsx', 'cva'],
  tailwindAttributes: ['className', 'class'],

  // Import sorting
  importOrder: [
    '<BUILTIN_MODULES>',
    '',
    '^react$',
    '^next(/.*)?$',
    '',
    '<THIRD_PARTY_MODULES>',
    '',
    '^@/(.*)$',
    '',
    '^[./]',
  ],
  importOrderSeparation: true,
  importOrderSortSpecifiers: true,
  importOrderTypeScriptVersion: '5.0.0',
};
```

**Install:**
```bash
npm install -D prettier prettier-plugin-tailwindcss @ianvs/prettier-plugin-sort-imports
```

**package.json scripts:**
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "format": "prettier --write .",
    "format:check": "prettier --check ."
  }
}
```

The `tailwindFunctions` option tells the plugin to also sort classes inside `cn()`, `clsx()`, and `cva()` calls, not just JSX `className` attributes. Import order groups are separated by empty strings which become blank lines.

title: pnpm workspaces setup for monorepo package management
body:
pnpm workspaces provide efficient monorepo package management with a single lockfile and symlinked dependencies. Faster and more disk-efficient than npm/yarn workspaces.

```yaml
# pnpm-workspace.yaml
packages:
  - 'apps/*'
  - 'packages/*'
```

**Monorepo structure:**
```
my-monorepo/
├── pnpm-workspace.yaml
├── package.json
├── turbo.json
├── apps/
│   ├── web/
│   │   └── package.json
│   └── api/
│       └── package.json
└── packages/
    ├── ui/
    │   └── package.json
    ├── shared/
    │   └── package.json
    └── tsconfig/
        └── package.json
```

**Root package.json:**
```json
{
  "name": "my-monorepo",
  "private": true,
  "scripts": {
    "build": "turbo run build",
    "dev": "turbo run dev",
    "lint": "turbo run lint"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "turbo": "^2.0.0"
  },
  "packageManager": "pnpm@9.0.0"
}
```

**Package referencing internal packages:**
```json
{
  "name": "@myapp/web",
  "dependencies": {
    "@myapp/ui": "workspace:*",
    "@myapp/shared": "workspace:*"
  }
}
```

**Common pnpm workspace commands:**
```bash
# Install all dependencies
pnpm install

# Add dependency to a specific package
pnpm add react --filter @myapp/web

# Add dev dependency to root
pnpm add -Dw typescript

# Run script in specific package
pnpm --filter @myapp/web dev

# Run in all packages that match
pnpm --filter "@myapp/*" build
```

`workspace:*` resolves to the local package during development and the published version when published. pnpm's content-addressable store means identical dependencies across packages are stored only once on disk.

title: Changesets workflow for versioning and publishing monorepo packages
body:
Changesets manages versioning, changelogs, and publishing for monorepo packages. Each PR adds a changeset describing the changes.

```bash
# Install
pnpm add -Dw @changesets/cli
pnpm changeset init
```

**.changeset/config.json:**
```json
{
  "$schema": "https://unpkg.com/@changesets/config@3.0.0/schema.json",
  "changelog": "@changesets/cli/changelog",
  "commit": false,
  "fixed": [],
  "linked": [["@myapp/ui", "@myapp/shared"]],
  "access": "restricted",
  "baseBranch": "main",
  "updateInternalDependencies": "patch",
  "ignore": ["@myapp/web", "@myapp/api"]
}
```

**Create a changeset:**
```bash
pnpm changeset
# Interactive prompt:
# ? Which packages would you like to include? @myapp/ui
# ? What type of change? minor
# ? Summary: Add new Button variants for outline and ghost styles
```

This creates `.changeset/fuzzy-dogs-cry.md`:
```markdown
---
'@myapp/ui': minor
---

Add new Button variants for outline and ghost styles
```

**Version and publish:**
```bash
# Apply changesets: bump versions, update changelogs
pnpm changeset version

# Review changes
git diff

# Publish to npm
pnpm changeset publish

# Push tags
git push --follow-tags
```

**CI integration (GitHub Actions):**
```yaml
- uses: changesets/action@v1
  with:
    publish: pnpm changeset publish
    version: pnpm changeset version
  env:
    GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
    NPM_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
```

`linked` packages are versioned together. `ignore` skips apps that aren't published. The GitHub Action creates a "Version Packages" PR automatically.

title: Turborepo vs Nx: monorepo build tool comparison
body:
Both tools optimize monorepo builds with caching and task orchestration. They target different complexity levels.

**Turborepo** — simple, convention-based:
```json
// turbo.json
{
  "tasks": {
    "build": {
      "dependsOn": ["^build"],
      "outputs": ["dist/**"]
    }
  }
}
```

```bash
turbo run build --filter=@myapp/web...
```

**Nx** — feature-rich, plugin ecosystem:
```json
// nx.json
{
  "targetDefaults": {
    "build": {
      "dependsOn": ["^build"],
      "cache": true
    }
  },
  "plugins": ["@nx/next", "@nx/react"]
}
```

```bash
nx run web:build
nx affected --target=build  # Only build what changed
nx graph                    # Visualize dependency graph
```

**Comparison:**
| Feature | Turborepo | Nx |
|---------|-----------|----|
| Setup complexity | Minimal | Medium |
| Config file | turbo.json | nx.json + project.json |
| Remote caching | Vercel (free) | Nx Cloud (free tier) |
| Affected commands | --filter | nx affected |
| Code generation | No | Yes (generators) |
| Dependency graph | No | Yes (visual) |
| Plugins | No | Rich ecosystem |
| CI distribution | No | Yes (Nx Agents) |
| Module federation | No | Yes |
| Learning curve | Low | Medium-High |

**Choose Turborepo** for simpler monorepos, Vercel-deployed projects, and teams that prefer minimal tooling. **Choose Nx** for large monorepos (100+ packages), enterprise teams needing code generators, and projects wanting CI distribution.

title: Git rebase vs merge: when rebase causes lost commits
body:
Rebasing public/shared branches can cause teammates to lose commits or create duplicate commits. Understanding when to use each prevents disasters.

**Safe: Rebase local/private branches onto main:**
```bash
# Update your feature branch with latest main
git checkout feature/my-work
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main
# Resolve conflicts if any
git push --force-with-lease  # Safe force push for your branch
```

**Dangerous: Rebase shared branches:**
```bash
# NEVER DO THIS if others have commits on this branch
git checkout develop
git rebase main
git push --force  # DESTROYS other people's work
```

**What goes wrong:** When you rebase, Git creates new commits with new SHAs. If teammate Alice pushed commit `abc` to the branch and you rebase, `abc` is replaced with `abc'` (same changes, different SHA). When Alice pulls, Git sees divergent history and creates merge conflicts or duplicate commits.

**Use `--force-with-lease` instead of `--force`:**
```bash
# Only force-pushes if remote hasn't changed since last fetch
git push --force-with-lease

# If someone pushed while you were rebasing:
# ! [rejected] feature -> feature (stale info)
# This protects you from overwriting their work
```

**Rule of thumb:**
- Rebase: your own feature branches before merge
- Merge: shared branches, main/develop, release branches
- `--force-with-lease`: always, instead of `--force`
- Never rebase commits that exist on a remote branch others pull from

title: Git aliases for common developer workflows
body:
Git aliases speed up daily operations. Add these to `~/.gitconfig` under `[alias]`.

```ini
[alias]
    # Status and log
    s = status -sb
    lg = log --oneline --graph --decorate -20
    lga = log --oneline --graph --decorate --all -30
    last = log -1 HEAD --stat

    # Branching
    co = checkout
    cb = checkout -b
    br = branch --sort=-committerdate --format='%(color:yellow)%(refname:short)%(color:reset) - %(color:green)%(committerdate:relative)%(color:reset) - %(contents:subject)'
    gone = "!git branch -vv | grep ': gone]' | awk '{print $1}'"
    prune-merged = "!git gone | xargs -r git branch -d"

    # Committing
    cm = commit -m
    ca = commit --amend --no-edit
    unstage = reset HEAD --
    undo = reset --soft HEAD~1

    # Stashing
    ss = stash push -m
    sp = stash pop
    sl = stash list

    # Working with remotes
    fp = fetch --prune
    publish = "!git push -u origin $(git branch --show-current)"

    # Diff
    d = diff --stat
    dc = diff --cached --stat
    dw = diff --word-diff

    # Find
    find-commit = log --all --oneline --grep
    find-file = "!git ls-files | grep -i"
    who = shortlog -sn --no-merges
```

**Usage:**
```bash
git s          # Short status
git lg         # Pretty log graph
git cb feature/new  # Create and switch to new branch
git cm "fix: resolve login issue"  # Quick commit
git undo       # Undo last commit (keep changes)
git publish    # Push current branch to origin
git gone       # List branches deleted on remote
git prune-merged  # Delete local branches merged on remote
```

The `gone` alias finds branches whose remote tracking branch was deleted (after PR merge). `prune-merged` cleans them up safely.

title: Nx monorepo setup with Next.js and shared TypeScript libraries
Semantic versioning with conventional commits and automated releases
body:
Conventional Commits standardize commit messages to enable automated version bumping and changelog generation.

**Commit format:**
```
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

**Types and their version impact:**
```bash
# PATCH (0.0.x)
fix: resolve null pointer in user lookup
perf: optimize database query for listing

# MINOR (0.x.0)
feat: add password reset flow
feat(auth): implement OAuth2 provider

# MAJOR (x.0.0)
feat!: redesign authentication API
feat: add new login flow

BREAKING CHANGE: The /login endpoint now requires MFA token
```

**Enforce with commitlint:**
```bash
npm install -D @commitlint/cli @commitlint/config-conventional
```

```javascript
// commitlint.config.js
export default {
  extends: ['@commitlint/config-conventional'],
  rules: {
    'type-enum': [2, 'always', [
      'feat', 'fix', 'docs', 'style', 'refactor',
      'perf', 'test', 'build', 'ci', 'chore', 'revert',
    ]],
    'subject-max-length': [2, 'always', 72],
    'body-max-line-length': [2, 'always', 100],
  },
};
```

**Git hook with husky:**
```bash
npm install -D husky
npx husky init
echo 'npx --no -- commitlint --edit "$1"' > .husky/commit-msg
```

**Automated release with semantic-release:**
```json
{
  "release": {
    "branches": ["main"],
    "plugins": [
      "@semantic-release/commit-analyzer",
      "@semantic-release/release-notes-generator",
      "@semantic-release/changelog",
      "@semantic-release/npm",
      "@semantic-release/github",
      "@semantic-release/git"
    ]
  }
}
```

`fix:` bumps patch, `feat:` bumps minor, `BREAKING CHANGE:` or `!` bumps major. This chain (conventional commits + commitlint + semantic-release) fully automates versioning.

title: GitHub Actions CI pipeline for monorepo with Turborepo caching
body:
A CI pipeline that leverages Turborepo's remote caching and runs only affected tasks for faster monorepo builds.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

concurrency:
  group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }}
  cancel-in-progress: true

jobs:
  ci:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0  # Needed for Turborepo affected detection

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          cache: 'pnpm'

      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile

      - name: Lint
        run: pnpm turbo run lint --filter='...[origin/main...HEAD]'

      - name: Type check
        run: pnpm turbo run typecheck --filter='...[origin/main...HEAD]'

      - name: Test
        run: pnpm turbo run test --filter='...[origin/main...HEAD]'

      - name: Build
        run: pnpm turbo run build
        env:
          TURBO_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.TURBO_TOKEN }}
          TURBO_TEAM: ${{ vars.TURBO_TEAM }}

      - name: Upload coverage
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: coverage
          path: '**/coverage/lcov.info'
```

**Key optimizations:**
- `concurrency` cancels previous runs on the same PR
- `fetch-depth: 0` enables Turborepo to compare against main
- `--filter='...[origin/main...HEAD]'` runs only for packages changed since main
- `TURBO_TOKEN` enables remote caching (shared between CI and developers)
- `--frozen-lockfile` ensures reproducible installs

For large monorepos, this reduces CI time from 15+ minutes to 2-3 minutes for typical PRs that touch 1-2 packages.

title: ESLint flat config errors when migrating from legacy format
Husky and lint-staged for pre-commit hooks in modern projects
body:
Husky manages Git hooks and lint-staged runs linters only on staged files for fast pre-commit checks.

```bash
# Install
npm install -D husky lint-staged
npx husky init
```

**package.json:**
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "prepare": "husky"
  },
  "lint-staged": {
    "*.{ts,tsx}": [
      "eslint --fix",
      "prettier --write"
    ],
    "*.{json,md,yml,yaml}": [
      "prettier --write"
    ],
    "*.css": [
      "prettier --write"
    ]
  }
}
```

**.husky/pre-commit:**
```bash
pnpm lint-staged
```

**.husky/commit-msg:**
```bash
npx --no -- commitlint --edit "$1"
```

**For monorepos with Turborepo:**
```json
{
  "lint-staged": {
    "*.{ts,tsx}": [
      "bash -c 'pnpm turbo run typecheck --filter=...[HEAD]'",
      "eslint --fix",
      "prettier --write"
    ]
  }
}
```

**Skip hooks when needed:**
```bash
# Emergency — skip all hooks
git commit --no-verify -m "hotfix: critical production fix"

# Better — skip specific hooks via env var
HUSKY=0 git commit -m "wip: work in progress"
```

lint-staged automatically stages fixes made by ESLint and Prettier. This means `eslint --fix` changes are included in the commit. For TypeScript type-checking, run it on the whole project (not per-file) since types depend on other files.

title: Git stash pop conflicts leave stash entry intact
body:
When `git stash pop` encounters merge conflicts, the stash entry is NOT dropped. Many developers don't realize this and end up with stale stash entries or re-applying the same stash.

```bash
# Apply and remove stash (normally)
git stash pop
# Success: stash is applied AND removed from stash list

# But with conflicts:
git stash pop
# Auto-merging file.ts
# CONFLICT (content): Merge conflict in file.ts
# The stash IS NOT dropped — it stays in the stash list!

# Check — stash is still there:
git stash list
# stash@{0}: WIP on main: abc1234 previous commit

# After resolving conflicts manually:
git add file.ts
git commit -m "resolve stash conflicts"

# NOW manually drop the stash:
git stash drop stash@{0}
```

**Safe workflow:**
```bash
# Use 'apply' instead of 'pop' to be explicit
git stash apply
# Resolve any conflicts
git add .
# Only drop after confirming changes are correct
git stash drop
```

**Other stash tips:**
```bash
# Stash with a descriptive name
git stash push -m "WIP: login form validation"

# Stash specific files
git stash push -m "api changes" src/api/

# Stash including untracked files
git stash push -u -m "including new files"

# View stash contents without applying
git stash show -p stash@{0}

# Create a branch from a stash
git stash branch feature/from-stash stash@{0}
```

Using `apply` + explicit `drop` is safer than `pop` because you can verify the changes were applied correctly before removing the stash entry.

title: GitHub Actions reusable workflows for DRY CI/CD pipelines
body:
Reusable workflows define CI logic once and call it from multiple repositories or workflow files. Inputs and secrets are passed explicitly.

**Reusable workflow definition:**
```yaml
# .github/workflows/reusable-deploy.yml
name: Reusable Deploy

on:
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      environment:
        required: true
        type: string
      node-version:
        required: false
        type: string
        default: '20'
    secrets:
      DEPLOY_TOKEN:
        required: true
    outputs:
      deploy-url:
        description: 'Deployment URL'
        value: ${{ jobs.deploy.outputs.url }}

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: ${{ inputs.environment }}
    outputs:
      url: ${{ steps.deploy.outputs.url }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }}
          cache: 'pnpm'
      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm build
      - name: Deploy
        id: deploy
        run: |
          URL=$(deploy-command --token ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_TOKEN }})
          echo "url=$URL" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
```

**Caller workflow:**
```yaml
# .github/workflows/deploy-production.yml
name: Deploy Production

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy-staging:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-deploy.yml
    with:
      environment: staging
    secrets:
      DEPLOY_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.STAGING_DEPLOY_TOKEN }}

  deploy-production:
    needs: deploy-staging
    uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-deploy.yml
    with:
      environment: production
    secrets:
      DEPLOY_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PROD_DEPLOY_TOKEN }}

  notify:
    needs: deploy-production
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: echo "Deployed to ${{ needs.deploy-production.outputs.deploy-url }}"
```

Reusable workflows can reference other repos: `uses: myorg/shared-workflows/.github/workflows/deploy.yml@main`. Secrets must be explicitly passed — they don't inherit automatically.

title: How to handle large file tracking in Git with Git LFS
body:
Git LFS (Large File Storage) replaces large files with text pointers and stores file contents on a remote server. Essential for repos with binaries, images, or media.

```bash
# Install Git LFS
brew install git-lfs  # macOS
git lfs install       # Enable in current user

# Track file types
git lfs track "*.psd"
git lfs track "*.zip"
git lfs track "*.mp4"
git lfs track "assets/fonts/*.woff2"

# This creates/updates .gitattributes
cat .gitattributes
# *.psd filter=lfs diff=lfs merge=lfs -text
# *.zip filter=lfs diff=lfs merge=lfs -text

# IMPORTANT: commit .gitattributes first
git add .gitattributes
git commit -m "chore: configure Git LFS tracking"

# Then add and commit large files normally
git add assets/design.psd
git commit -m "add design file"
git push
```

**Verify LFS is working:**
```bash
# List tracked patterns
git lfs track

# List LFS-managed files
git lfs ls-files

# Check LFS status
git lfs status

# Show pointer file content
git show HEAD:assets/design.psd
# version https://git-lfs.github.com/spec/v1
# oid sha256:abc123...
# size 15728640
```

**Migrate existing files to LFS:**
```bash
# Migrate existing committed files (rewrites history!)
git lfs migrate import --include="*.psd" --everything

# For specific paths
git lfs migrate import --include="assets/videos/**" --everything

# Force push after migration
git push --force-with-lease --all
```

**Limits:** GitHub LFS has 1GB free storage and 1GB/month bandwidth. Additional storage is $5/50GB/month. GitLab includes 10GB LFS per project on free tier.

title: Semantic versioning rules and when to bump major, minor, patch
body:
Semantic Versioning (SemVer) uses a `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` format. Each segment communicates intent about backward compatibility.

**Version format: `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH` (e.g., `2.4.1`)**

```
MAJOR — Breaking changes (incompatible API changes)
MINOR — New features (backward-compatible additions)
PATCH — Bug fixes (backward-compatible fixes)
```

**Examples:**
```bash
# PATCH bump: 1.2.3 → 1.2.4
# - Fix null pointer exception in login
# - Correct typo in error message
# - Performance improvement (no API change)

# MINOR bump: 1.2.3 → 1.3.0 (reset patch to 0)
# - Add new /users/search endpoint
# - Add optional 'format' parameter to existing endpoint
# - Deprecate old method (still works, just logged warning)

# MAJOR bump: 1.2.3 → 2.0.0 (reset minor and patch to 0)
# - Remove deprecated /v1/users endpoint
# - Change response format from XML to JSON
# - Rename exported function from getUserById to findUser
# - Require new mandatory parameter
```

**Pre-release versions:**
```bash
1.0.0-alpha.1    # Alpha: unstable, may change
1.0.0-beta.1     # Beta: feature-complete, may have bugs
1.0.0-rc.1       # Release candidate: ready unless bugs found
```

**Range specifiers in package.json:**
```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "^1.2.3": ">=1.2.3 <2.0.0",
    "~1.2.3": ">=1.2.3 <1.3.0",
    "1.2.3":  "exactly 1.2.3"
  }
}
```

**0.x.y special rules:** Before 1.0.0, the public API is not stable. `0.MINOR.PATCH` — minor bumps may contain breaking changes. This is why `^0.2.3` resolves to `>=0.2.3 <0.3.0` (not `<1.0.0`).

title: Git hooks with lefthook as a faster alternative to Husky
body:
Lefthook is a Git hooks manager written in Go. It's faster than Husky+lint-staged and supports parallel execution.

```bash
# Install
npm install -D lefthook
npx lefthook install
```

```yaml
# lefthook.yml
pre-commit:
  parallel: true
  commands:
    lint:
      glob: "*.{ts,tsx}"
      run: npx eslint --fix {staged_files}
      stage_fixed: true  # Auto-stage fixed files
    format:
      glob: "*.{ts,tsx,json,md,css}"
      run: npx prettier --write {staged_files}
      stage_fixed: true
    typecheck:
      glob: "*.{ts,tsx}"
      run: npx tsc --noEmit

commit-msg:
  commands:
    commitlint:
      run: npx commitlint --edit {1}

pre-push:
  commands:
    test:
      run: npx vitest run --changed HEAD~1
```

**Monorepo configuration:**
```yaml
# lefthook.yml
pre-commit:
  parallel: true
  commands:
    lint-web:
      root: "apps/web/"
      glob: "*.{ts,tsx}"
      run: npx eslint --fix {staged_files}
      stage_fixed: true
    lint-api:
      root: "apps/api/"
      glob: "*.ts"
      run: npx eslint --fix {staged_files}
      stage_fixed: true
    format:
      run: npx prettier --write {staged_files}
      stage_fixed: true
```

**Why lefthook over Husky + lint-staged:**
- Single config file (no `.husky/` directory)
- Parallel command execution built-in
- `{staged_files}` variable replaces lint-staged
- `stage_fixed: true` auto-stages linter fixes
- Written in Go — faster startup than Node-based tools
- `root` option for monorepo scoping

title: How to recover lost commits after accidental git reset or branch deletion
body:
Git reflog records all HEAD movements, including resets and branch deletions. Lost commits can be recovered for at least 90 days.

**Recover after `git reset --hard`:**
```bash
# See all recent HEAD positions
git reflog
# abc1234 HEAD@{0}: reset: moving to HEAD~3
# def5678 HEAD@{1}: commit: add payment integration
# ghi9012 HEAD@{2}: commit: add checkout flow
# jkl3456 HEAD@{3}: commit: add cart component

# Recover — create a branch at the lost commit
git branch recovery def5678
git checkout recovery

# Or reset back to where you were
git reset --hard def5678
```

**Recover a deleted branch:**
```bash
# Find the branch tip in reflog
git reflog | grep "feature/payments"
# def5678 HEAD@{5}: checkout: moving from feature/payments to main

# Recreate the branch
git branch feature/payments def5678
```

**Recover after `git stash drop`:**
```bash
# Find unreachable stash commits
git fsck --unreachable | grep commit
# unreachable commit mno7890

# Inspect it
git show mno7890

# Apply it
git stash apply mno7890
```

**Nuclear recovery — find ALL dangling commits:**
```bash
# List all commits not reachable from any branch
git fsck --lost-found
# Commits are saved to .git/lost-found/commit/

# Browse them
ls .git/lost-found/commit/
git show <sha>
```

**Prevention:**
```bash
# Before destructive operations, tag current state
git tag backup/before-rebase
git rebase -i main
# If something goes wrong:
git reset --hard backup/before-rebase
git tag -d backup/before-rebase
```

Reflog entries expire after 90 days by default (`gc.reflogExpire`). Run `git gc` cautiously — it can remove unreachable objects.

title: Biome vs ESLint+Prettier: all-in-one formatting and linting
pnpm peer dependency resolution differences from npm and yarn
body:
pnpm enforces strict peer dependency resolution by default, which causes install failures that npm silently ignores.

**Error:**
```
 ERR_PNPM_PEER_DEP_ISSUES  Unmet peer dependencies

├── eslint-plugin-react@7.35.0
│   └── ✕ unmet peer eslint@"^7.0.0 || ^8.0.0": found 9.0.0
└── @typescript-eslint/parser@7.0.0
    └── ✕ unmet peer eslint@"^8.0.0": found 9.0.0
```

**Fix 1 — Update the offending packages:**
```bash
pnpm update eslint-plugin-react @typescript-eslint/parser
```

**Fix 2 — Allow auto-install of missing peers:**
```ini
# .npmrc
auto-install-peers=true
```

**Fix 3 — Override peer dependency resolution:**
```json
// package.json
{
  "pnpm": {
    "peerDependencyRules": {
      "allowedVersions": {
        "eslint": "9"
      },
      "ignoreMissing": [
        "@babel/core"
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

**Fix 4 — Legacy peer deps mode (not recommended):**
```ini
# .npmrc
strict-peer-dependencies=false
```

**Why pnpm is strict:** npm hoists all dependencies to a flat `node_modules`, accidentally making undeclared dependencies importable. pnpm uses a content-addressable store with symlinks, so only declared dependencies are importable. This catches real bugs: if your code imports a package not in your `package.json`, it fails in pnpm but silently works in npm.

**The `.npmrc` file for most pnpm projects:**
```ini
auto-install-peers=true
shamefully-hoist=false
```

title: Turborepo cache miss causes: understanding cache key composition
Git maintenance commands for keeping repositories fast
body:
Large Git repositories slow down over time. Regular maintenance keeps operations fast. Git 2.29+ includes a built-in maintenance scheduler.

```bash
# Enable automatic maintenance (recommended)
git maintenance start
# Registers a cron job / launchd plist for automatic optimization

# Manual maintenance tasks
git maintenance run --task=gc           # Garbage collection
git maintenance run --task=commit-graph # Speed up log/graph operations
git maintenance run --task=prefetch     # Background fetch
git maintenance run --task=loose-objects # Pack loose objects
git maintenance run --task=incremental-repack  # Optimize pack files
```

**Repository diagnostics:**
```bash
# Check repository health
git fsck --full

# Show repository size
git count-objects -vH
# count: 0
# size: 0 bytes
# in-pack: 45230
# packs: 1
# size-pack: 124.5 MiB

# Find large objects
git rev-list --objects --all | \
  git cat-file --batch-check='%(objecttype) %(objectname) %(objectsize) %(rest)' | \
  sed -n 's/^blob //p' | \
  sort -rnk2 | \
  head -10
```

**Optimize clone for large repos:**
```bash
# Shallow clone (only latest commit)
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/org/repo.git

# Partial clone (download blobs on demand)
git clone --filter=blob:none https://github.com/org/repo.git

# Sparse checkout (only specific directories)
git clone --filter=blob:none --sparse https://github.com/org/repo.git
cd repo
git sparse-checkout set apps/web packages/shared
```

`git maintenance start` is the single best optimization. It runs garbage collection, updates the commit-graph, and repacks objects on a schedule. For CI, `--filter=blob:none` dramatically reduces clone time by only downloading blobs when accessed.

title: Trunk-based development branching strategy with short-lived feature branches
body:
Trunk-based development (TBD) keeps the main branch always deployable. Feature branches are short-lived (1-2 days max) and merged frequently.

```bash
# Start a feature (from latest main)
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git checkout -b feature/add-search

# Work in small increments, commit often
git add .
git commit -m "feat: add search input component"
git commit -m "feat: add search API endpoint"
git commit -m "test: add search integration tests"

# Keep branch updated with main (daily)
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main

# Push and create PR (aim for <1 day)
git push -u origin feature/add-search
gh pr create --title "feat: add search functionality"

# After review, squash merge
gh pr merge --squash

# Clean up
git checkout main
git pull
git branch -d feature/add-search
```

**Feature flags for incomplete features:**
```typescript
// Deploy incomplete features behind flags
if (featureFlags.isEnabled('search-v2')) {
  return <NewSearch />;
}
return <LegacySearch />;
```

**Branch naming convention:**
```
feature/short-description   # New features
fix/issue-number-description # Bug fixes
chore/description            # Maintenance
hotfix/description           # Urgent production fixes
```

**Comparison with GitFlow:**
| Aspect | Trunk-Based | GitFlow |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| Branch lifetime | Hours-1 day | Days-weeks |
| Merge conflicts | Rare | Common |
| Deploy frequency | Continuous | Scheduled |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Best for | SaaS, web apps | Mobile, versioned software |

Trunk-based works best with: CI/CD pipelines, feature flags, comprehensive test suites, and code review culture. The key discipline is keeping branches short-lived to minimize merge conflicts.

title: How to set up path aliases in TypeScript monorepo shared across packages
GitHub Actions matrix strategy for testing across Node versions and OS
body:
Matrix strategies run a job across multiple configurations in parallel. Useful for testing compatibility across Node versions, operating systems, and package managers.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/test.yml
name: Test

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false  # Don't cancel other jobs if one fails
      matrix:
        node-version: [18, 20, 22]
        os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]
        exclude:
          - os: windows-latest
            node-version: 18  # Skip Node 18 on Windows
        include:
          - os: ubuntu-latest
            node-version: 20
            coverage: true  # Only collect coverage for one combo

    runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
    name: Node ${{ matrix.node-version }} on ${{ matrix.os }}

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4
        with:
          version: 9

      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
          cache: 'pnpm'

      - run: pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
      - run: pnpm test

      - name: Upload coverage
        if: matrix.coverage
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
        with:
          token: ${{ secrets.CODECOV_TOKEN }}
          files: coverage/lcov.info

  test-complete:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: always()
    steps:
      - name: Check test results
        run: |
          if [ "${{ needs.test.result }}" = "failure" ]; then
            echo "Tests failed"
            exit 1
          fi
```

`fail-fast: false` ensures all matrix combinations run even if one fails — important for identifying platform-specific issues. `exclude` removes specific combinations. `include` adds extra properties to specific combinations. The `test-complete` job provides a single required status check for branch protection.
